name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (٣٤)
```

```english
Indeed for the Godwary there will be gardens of bliss near their Lord. (34)
```

```arabic
أَفَنَجْعَلُ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ كَٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٣٥)
```

```english
Shall We treat those who submit [to Us] as [We treat] the guilty? (35)
```

```arabic
مَا لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ (٣٦)
```

```english
What is the matter with you? How do you judge! (36)
```

```arabic
أَمْ لَكُمْ كِتَـٰبٌ فِيهِ تَدْرُسُونَ (٣٧)
```

```english
Do you possess a scripture in which you read (37)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ لَكُمْ فِيهِ لَمَا تَخَيَّرُونَ (٣٨)
```

```english
that you shall have in it whatever you choose? (38)
```

```arabic
أَمْ لَكُمْ أَيْمَـٰنٌ عَلَيْنَا بَـٰلِغَةٌ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۙ إِنَّ لَكُمْ لَمَا تَحْكُمُونَ (٣٩)
```

```english
Do you have a pledge binding on Us until the Day of Resurrection, that you shall indeed have whatever you decide? (39)
```

```arabic
سَلْهُمْ أَيُّهُم بِذَٰلِكَ زَعِيمٌ (٤٠)
```

```english
Ask them, which of them will aver [any of] that! (40)
```

```arabic
أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكَآءُ فَلْيَأْتُوا۟ بِشُرَكَآئِهِمْ إِن كَانُوا۟ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٤١)
```

```english
Do they have any ‘partners’ [that they claim for Allah]? Then let them produce their partners, if they are truthful. (41)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَن سَاقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلسُّجُودِ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ (٤٢)
```

```english
The day when the catastrophe occurs and they are summoned to prostrate themselves, they will not be able [to do it]. (42)
```

```arabic
خَـٰشِعَةً أَبْصَـٰرُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ ۖ وَقَدْ كَانُوا۟ يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى ٱلسُّجُودِ وَهُمْ سَـٰلِمُونَ (٤٣)
```

```english
With a humbled look [in their eyes], they will be overcast by abasement. Certainly, they were summoned to prostrate themselves while they were yet sound. (43)
```

```arabic
فَذَرْنِى وَمَن يُكَذِّبُ بِهَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ ۖ سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُم مِّنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٤٤)
```

```english
So leave Me with those who deny this discourse. We will draw them imperceptibly [into ruin], whence they do not know. (44)
```

```arabic
وَأُمْلِى لَهُمْ ۚ إِنَّ كَيْدِى مَتِينٌ (٤٥)
```

```english
I will grant them respite, for My devising is indeed sure. (45)
```

```arabic
أَمْ تَسْـَٔلُهُمْ أَجْرًا فَهُم مِّن مَّغْرَمٍ مُّثْقَلُونَ (٤٦)
```

```english
Do you ask them for a reward, so that they are weighed down with debt? (46)
```

```arabic
أَمْ عِندَهُمُ ٱلْغَيْبُ فَهُمْ يَكْتُبُونَ (٤٧)
```

```english
Do they possess [access to] the Unseen, so that they write it down? (47)
```

```arabic
فَٱصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ وَلَا تَكُن كَصَاحِبِ ٱلْحُوتِ إِذْ نَادَىٰ وَهُوَ مَكْظُومٌ (٤٨)
```

```english
So submit patiently to the judgement of your Lord, and do not be like the Man of the Fish who called out as he choked with grief. (48)
```

```arabic
لَّوْلَآ أَن تَدَٰرَكَهُۥ نِعْمَةٌ مِّن رَّبِّهِۦ لَنُبِذَ بِٱلْعَرَآءِ وَهُوَ مَذْمُومٌ (٤٩)
```

```english
Had it not been for a blessing that came to his rescue from his Lord, he would surely have been cast on the bare shore, being blameworthy. (49)
```

```arabic
فَٱجْتَبَـٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَجَعَلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٥٠)
```

```english
So his Lord chose him and made him one of the righteous. (50)
```

```arabic
وَإِن يَكَادُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ بِأَبْصَـٰرِهِمْ لَمَّا سَمِعُوا۟ ٱلذِّكْرَ وَيَقُولُونَ إِنَّهُۥ لَمَجْنُونٌ (٥١)
```

```english
Indeed the faithless almost devour you with their eyes when they hear this Reminder, and they say, ‘He is indeed crazy.’ (51)
```

```arabic
وَمَا هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٥٢)
```

```english
Yet it is just a reminder for all the nations. (52)
```

## (Commentary)

This section serves as a conclusion to the preceding warnings addressed to those who deny the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and as a formal recording of the punishment decreed for them in the Hereafter, for the God-conscious (المتقون) — *those who possess godwariness (التقوى) by guarding their souls against Allah's displeasure* — shall be in gardens of bliss. It establishes, through a decisive proof, that they and the God-conscious are not equal; thus, the criminals (المجرمون) have no right to hope for honor from Allah while they remain in their state of criminality. Consequently, the worldly blessings they experience are merely a process of gradual enticement (استدراج) and a granting of respite (إملاء).

Furthermore, it reinforces the command to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to remain patient with the decree of his Lord.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ** — **Indeed for the God-conscious there will be gardens of bliss with their Lord.** (68:34). This is glad tidings and an exposition of the state of the God-conscious in the Hereafter, in contrast to what was previously explained regarding the state of the deniers therein.

In His saying, "with their Lord" (عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ), rather than saying "with Allah," there is an allusion to the bond of Lordship (التدبير) — *the divine management of affairs* — and mercy between them and Him, the Glorified. It indicates that this reward is granted to them in exchange for their restricting Lordship to Him, the Exalted, and their sincere devotion in servitude (العبودية) to Him.

The annexation of "gardens" (الجنات) to "bliss" (النعيم) — which refers to favor or blessing (النعمة) — is to indicate that everything therein is a pure blessing untainted by any affliction, and a pleasure unmixed with pain. It will be mentioned, Allah willing, in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ لَتُسْئَلُنَّ يَوْمَئِذٍ عَنِ النَّعِيمِ** — **Then, on that day, you will surely be questioned about the bliss** (102:8), that "bliss" (النعيم) refers to the *Wilayah* (الولاية) — *the divine authority and guardianship*.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَنَجْعَلُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ كَالْمُجْرِمِينَ** — **Shall We then treat those who have submitted as We treat the guilty?** (68:35). At first glance, this verse may be interpreted as an argument for the Resurrection (المعاد), similar to His saying, the Exalted: **أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ** — **Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious?** (38:28), the commentary of which has already preceded.

Alternatively, it could be a rebuttal to those among them who said to the believers: "If there were to be a resurrection and a return, we would be blessed just as we are in this world." Allah, the Glorified, has recounted such a claim from one of them: **وَما أَظُنُّ السَّاعَةَ قائِمَةً وَلَئِنْ رُجِعْتُ إِلى رَبِّي إِنَّ لِي عِنْدَهُ لَلْحُسْنى** — **I do not think the Hour will ever set in, and even if I am returned to my Lord, I will indeed have the best with Him.** (41:50).

The apparent context of the subsequent verses, which refute their judgment of equality, favors the second possibility. This is supported by what has been narrated: when the polytheists heard the discourse regarding the Resurrection and the Return, they said: "If what Muhammad and those who believe with him say is true, our state will be nothing but better than theirs, just as it is in this world, or at the very least, our state and theirs will be equal."

However, an objection arises: if the verse were intended solely to refute their claim that "we will be equal to them in the Hereafter or exceed them as we do in this world," the correspondence between the rebuttal and the claim would have required saying: "Shall We then make the criminals like those who have submitted?" Yet, the phrasing is reversed.

Contemplation (التدبر) on the context reveals that the verse is indeed aimed at refuting their claim of equality, but not merely by denying that they, despite their criminality, are equal to those who have submitted. Rather, it goes further by indicating that the honor (الكرامة) of those who have submitted (المسلمين) precludes the criminals from being made equal to them. It is as if it were said: "Your claim that you and those who have submitted will be equal is false, for Allah does not permit that those who have submitted — given the honor they hold with Him — be treated like the criminals, and you are indeed criminals."

Thus, the verse establishes the proof for the lack of equality between the two groups based on its incompatibility with the honor of those who have submitted before Allah, the Exalted, rather than its incompatibility with His justice regarding the criminals.

What is meant by "submission" (الإسلام) is the surrendering of one's affairs to Allah, such that one follows nothing but what He, the Glorified, wills in terms of action or omission. Its opposite is "criminality" (الإجرام), which is the acquisition of sin and the refusal to submit.

This verse and those following it, up to His saying: **أَمْ عِنْدَهُمُ الْغَيْبُ فَهُمْ يَكْتُبُونَ** — **Do they possess [knowledge of] the Unseen, so they write it down?** (68:47), are in the position of refuting their judgment that the criminals and those who have submitted will be equal in state on the Day of Resurrection. These verses present the various possibilities for the source of this judgment in the form of rhetorical questions (استفهامات إنكارية) and then refute them.

The formalization of the argument is as follows: The criminals being like those who have submitted on the Day of Resurrection, as they have judged, must either be from Allah, the Exalted, as a gift and mercy, or not from Him.

If it is from Him, then either there is a rational proof (دليل العقل) for it — and there is no such proof, which is the meaning of His saying: **ما لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ** — **What is the matter with you? How do you judge?** (68:36).

Or there is a textual proof (النقل) for it — and there is none, which is His saying: **أَمْ لَكُمْ كِتابٌ** — **Or do you have a scripture...** (68:37) etc. Or it is neither by rational nor textual proof, but rather through a direct verbal encounter between them and Allah, the Glorified, in which they took a covenant and a pledge from Him to treat both groups equally — and this is not the case. These are three possibilities.

If it is not from Allah, then their judgment of equality is either a serious judgment or it is not. If it is serious, then the equality they judge must either be based on themselves — in that they possess the power to make their state on the Day of Resurrection like that of those who have submitted, even if Allah does not will it — and this is not the case, which is His saying: **سَلْهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ بِذلِكَ زَعِيمٌ** — **Ask them, which of them will guarantee that!** (68:40). Or, the one undertaking this matter and managing it are their "partners" (شركاؤهم), yet there are no such partners, which is His saying: **أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكاءُ فَلْيَأْتُوا بِشُرَكائِهِمْ** — **Or do they have partners? Then let them bring their partners...** (68:41) etc.

Or it is because the Unseen (الغيب) — *that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge* — is with them, and the matters that people will face, their measure (قدر) and decree (قضاء), are tied to their will, occurring as they write them down; thus, they wrote for themselves equality with those who have submitted. This is not the case, for they have no access to the Unseen, which is His saying: **أَمْ عِنْدَهُمُ الْغَيْبُ فَهُمْ يَكْتُبُونَ** — **Do they possess [knowledge of] the Unseen, so they write it down?** (68:47). These are another three possibilities.

If their judgment of equality is not a serious one, but rather they uttered this statement merely to escape and flee from following you in your call because you ask them for a reward (أجراً) for your message and your guiding them to the truth, such that they are burdened by the debt (مغرم) of it — this too is not the case, which is His saying: **أَمْ تَسْئَلُهُمْ أَجْراً فَهُمْ مِنْ مَغْرَمٍ مُثْقَلُونَ** — **Do you ask them for a reward, so they are overburdened by a debt?** (68:46).

This is the seventh of the possibilities.

This is what contemplation on the verses yields regarding the systematic categorization of the alternatives mentioned therein. Others have mentioned different ways of categorization; whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the more extensive commentaries.

Thus, His saying: **ما لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ** — **What is the matter with you? How do you judge?** (68:36) is intended to express wonder at their judgment that the criminals will be like those who have submitted on the Day of Resurrection. It is an allusion to the intellect's refusal to permit such equality. Its sum meaning is the negation of any rational judgment for this, as it means: "What has happened to you in terms of intellectual disorder and corruption of opinion that you have judged thus?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمْ لَكُمْ كِتابٌ فِيهِ تَدْرُسُونَ إِنَّ لَكُمْ فِيهِ لَما تَخَيَّرُونَ** — **Or do you have a scripture in which you study, that you shall have in it whatever you choose?** (68:37-38). This is an allusion to the absence of any textual proof for their judgment of equality, just as the previous verse alluded to its absence from a rational standpoint.

What is meant by "scripture" (الكتاب) is a heavenly book revealed from Allah that serves as a proof. "Studying" (درس) the book is reading it, and "choosing" (التخير) is selection. His saying: "that you shall have in it whatever you choose" (إِنَّ لَكُمْ فِيهِ لَما تَخَيَّرُونَ) stands in the position of the object for "you study," and the question is rhetorical.

The meaning is: "Or do you have a heavenly scripture in which you read that you shall have in the Hereafter — or absolutely — whatever you choose, and thus you have chosen happiness and Paradise?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمْ لَكُمْ أَيْمانٌ عَلَيْنا بالِغَةٌ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ إِنَّ لَكُمْ لَما تَحْكُمُونَ** — **Or do you have [the security of] any oaths from Us, reaching until the Day of Resurrection, that you shall indeed have whatever you judge?** (68:39). This is an allusion to the fact that they do not possess the right to judge based on a verbal covenant or oath given to them by Allah, the Glorified.

The word **أيمان** (oaths) is the plural of *yamin* (يمين), meaning a sworn oath. **البلوغ** (reaching/attaining) refers to reaching the ultimate degree of perfection; thus, "reaching oaths" (الأيمان البالغة) are those that are emphasized to the utmost degree. Based on this, the phrase "until the Day of Resurrection" (إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ) is a stable adverbial phrase (ظرف مستقر) related to an omitted word, the estimation being: "Or do you have against Us oaths existing until the Day of Resurrection, emphasized to the utmost degree..." etc.

It is also possible that "until the Day of Resurrection" is related to "reaching" (بالغة), where the "reaching" of the oaths refers to their applicability across the span of time until it ends at the Day of Resurrection.

Some have interpreted "oaths" (الأيمان) as covenants and pledges (العهود والمواثيق), which would be a case of mentioning the necessary consequence (the oath) while intending the thing necessitated (the covenant) as a metonymy (كناية), or it could be mentioning the part while intending the whole.

His saying: "that you shall indeed have whatever you judge" (إِنَّ لَكُمْ لَما تَحْكُمُونَ) is the response to the oath (جواب القسم), which is the matter covenanted upon, and the question is rhetorical.

The meaning is: "Or do you have against Us covenants in which We have sworn emphasized oaths until the Day of Resurrection that We have conceded to you that you shall have whatever you judge for yourselves?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سَلْهُمْ أَيُّهُمْ بِذلِكَ زَعِيمٌ** — **Ask them, which of them will guarantee that!** (68:40). This is a turning away (إعراض) from addressing them directly and a transition (التفات) to addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), because they have fallen below the level of deserving direct address. For this reason, the remaining four questions are presented in the third person (سياق الغيبة), the first of which is: "Ask them, which of them will guarantee that!" The **زعيم** (guarantor) is the one who stands by a matter and takes charge of it, and the question is rhetorical.

The meaning is: "Ask the polytheists which of them is in charge of this matter of equality that they claim? That is, if it is established that Allah does not treat the two groups equally due to the lack of any proof for it, then is the one who will undertake this matter and manage it one of them? Which one is it?" It is clear that such a notion is so absurd that none but one whose intellect is afflicted would utter it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكاءُ فَلْيَأْتُوا بِشُرَكائِهِمْ إِنْ كانُوا صادِقِينَ** — **Or do they have partners? Then let them bring their partners, if they are truthful.** (68:41). This is a rebuttal to them on the assumption that their judgment of equality is based on their claim that they have deities who share in Lordship (الربوبية) with Allah, the Glorified, and who will intercede for them with Allah to make them like those who have submitted. The question is rhetorical, serving to negate the existence of such partners.

His saying: "Then let them bring their partners..." etc., is a metonymy for the non-existence of these partners, serving to reinforce the negation contained in "Or do they have partners?"

It has been said that "partners" (الشركاء) refers to those who share their opinion. The meaning would then be: "Or do they have partners who share this view with them and follow their path? Then let them bring them if they are truthful." You are well aware that this meaning does not resolve the dispute.

It has also been said that "partners" refers to witnesses (الشهداء). The meaning would be: "Or do they have witnesses for this claim? Then let them bring them if they are truthful." This is an interpretation for which there is no evidence from the wording. Furthermore, it is redundant, for these witnesses would be witnessing either to a scripture from Allah or a promise by covenant and oath, both of which have already been refuted.

It has also been said that "partners" refers to partners in divinity (شركاء الألوهية) as they claim, but the meaning of "bringing them" is bringing them on the Day of Resurrection to testify for them or intercede for them with Allah, the Glorified. You are well aware that this meaning also does not resolve the dispute.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَنْ ساقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ** — **The day when the shank is bared, and they are summoned to prostrate, but they will not be able [to do it].** (68:42) — up to His saying — **وَهُمْ سالِمُونَ** — **while they were yet sound.** (68:43). "The day" (يوم) is an adverb related to an omitted verb such as "Remember" or the like. "Baring the shank" (الكشف عن الساق) is a metaphorical expression (تمثيل) for a matter becoming extremely severe, because people would tuck up their garments from their shanks when a matter became intense, whether for work or for flight. Al-Kashshaf states: "The meaning of 'the day when the shank is bared' is the day when the matter becomes severe and grave. There is no actual baring nor an actual shank, just as you say to a stingy person with a severed hand: 'his hand is shackled,' when there is neither a hand nor a shackle; it is merely a metaphor for stinginess." End quote.

This verse and the four following it are a parenthetical insertion (اعتراض) occasioned by the mention of their "partners" whom they claim will bring them happiness if there were to be a resurrection and reckoning. Allah, the Glorified, mentions that there are no partners for Allah and no intercession; rather, a human being only secures the happiness of the Hereafter through prostration (السجود) — that is, submission (الخضوع) to Allah, the Glorified, through the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) — in this world, so that he may carry the quality of submission with him and thus be happy on the Day of Resurrection.

These deniers and criminals did not prostrate to Allah in this world, so they will not be able to prostrate in the Hereafter. Thus, they will not be happy, and their state and the state of those who have submitted will never be equal therein. Rather, Allah, the Exalted, treats them in this world — due to their arrogance against prostrating to Him — with the treatment of gradual enticement (الاستدراج) and granting respite (الإملاء) until their wretchedness is complete, and they are returned to the painful punishment in the Hereafter.

Thus, His saying: **يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَنْ ساقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ فَلا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ** means: "Remember the day when the matter becomes severe for them and they are summoned to prostrate to Allah in submission, but they will not be able to do so because the trait of arrogance has become firmly rooted in their inner selves, and 'on the day when the secrets are examined' (1)."

His saying: **خاشِعَةً أَبْصارُهُمْ تَرْهَقُهُمْ ذِلَّةٌ** — **with their eyes humbled, they will be overcast by abasement.** (68:43). These are two circumstantial clauses (حالان) describing the subject of "they are summoned." That is, they will be in a state where their eyes are humbled and a state where abasement covers them by force. The attribution of humility to the eyes is because its effect is most apparent in them.

His saying: **وَقَدْ كانُوا يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ وَهُمْ سالِمُونَ** — **For they used to be summoned to prostrate while they were yet sound.** (68:43). What is meant by "soundness" (السلامة) is their freedom from the defects and maladies that afflicted their souls due to arrogance against the truth, which stripped them of the ability to respond to the truth; or it refers to their absolute capability for it in this world.

The meaning is: "They were summoned in the world to prostrate to Allah while they were sound and possessed the strongest capability for it, yet they did not respond."

It has been said that "prostration" (السجود) refers to the prayer (الصلاة), but this is as you see [weak].

---
(1) Surah al-Tariq, Ayah 9.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَذَرْنِي وَمَنْ يُكَذِّبُ بِهذَا الْحَدِيثِ** — **So leave Me to those who deny this discourse.** (68:44). What is meant by "this discourse" (هذا الحديث) is the Noble Quran. His saying: "So leave Me to those who deny..." etc., is a metonymy (كناية) meaning that He alone is sufficient to deal with them and that He will not leave them be. It contains a form of consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and a threat to the polytheists.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ** — **We will draw them on gradually from whence they do not know.** (68:44). This is a new sentence (استئناف) explaining the manner of His seizing them and punishing them, which was understood from His saying: "So leave Me..." etc.

**Gradual enticement** (الاستدراج) is to bring them down degree by degree until their wretchedness is complete and they fall into the pit of destruction. This occurs by Allah giving them blessing after blessing; whenever they are given a blessing, they become preoccupied with it, neglect to give thanks for it, increase in their forgetfulness of Him, and distance themselves from His remembrance.

Thus, *istidraj* is the giving of blessing after blessing, which causes them to descend degree by degree and approach the pit of destruction. Its being "from whence they do not know" is because it comes through the path of blessing, which they perceive as good and happiness, containing no evil or wretchedness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَأُمْلِي لَهُمْ إِنَّ كَيْدِي مَتِينٌ** — **And I will grant them respite; indeed My devising is sure.** (68:45). **الإملاء** (granting respite) is deferment. **الكيد** (devising/stratagem) is a type of subtle planning. **المتين** (sure/strong) means powerful.

The meaning is: "I will grant them time so they may indulge in Our blessings through acts of disobedience as they wish; indeed, My stratagem is powerful."

The subtle point in the transition (التفات) in "We will draw them on" (سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ) from the first-person singular to the first-person plural is to indicate greatness and that there are those appointed over these blessings that are poured upon them. The transition in His saying: "And I will grant them respite" (وَأُمْلِي لَهُمْ) from the plural back to the singular is because granting respite is a deferment of the appointed term (الأجل), and the matter of the term is never attributed in the Quran to anyone other than Allah, the Exalted. He said: **ثُمَّ قَضى أَجَلاً وَأَجَلٌ مُسَمًّى عِنْدَهُ** — **Then He decreed a term, and a term is fixed with Him.** (6:2).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمْ تَسْئَلُهُمْ أَجْراً فَهُمْ مِنْ مَغْرَمٍ مُثْقَلُونَ** — **Do you ask them for a reward, so they are overburdened by a debt?** (68:46). **المغرم** (debt) is a financial penalty or fine. **الإثقال** (overburdening) is the imposition of a heavy weight. The sentence is coordinated with His saying: "Or do they have partners?..." etc.

The meaning is: "Do you ask these criminals — who judge that the criminals and those who have submitted will be equal on the Day of Resurrection — for a reward for your call, such that they are overburdened by a fine you impose upon them, and thus they confront you with such a statement to escape the fine, without it being a serious statement from them?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَمْ عِنْدَهُمُ الْغَيْبُ فَهُمْ يَكْتُبُونَ** — **Do they possess [knowledge of] the Unseen, so they write it down?** (68:47). The apparent context suggests that what is meant by "the Unseen" (الغيب) is the Unseen of things, from which matters descend in limited measures and then settle upon the platform of manifestation. "Writing" (الكتابة) in this context refers to predetermination (التقدير) and decree (القضاء). The Unseen being "with them" refers to their dominance over it and their possession of it.

The meaning is: "Or is the matter of predetermination and decree in their hands, such that they decree as they wish, and thus they decree for themselves that they will be equal to those who have submitted on the Day of Resurrection?"

It has been said that the Unseen being "with them" refers to their knowledge of the correctness of what they have judged, and "writing" is taken in its literal sense. The meaning would be: "Or do they have knowledge of the correctness of what they claim, which is exclusive to them and unknown to others, so they write it down and pass it on as an inheritance, and they ought to produce it?"

This is far-fetched; rather, it is redundant, as the other mentioned possibilities suffice.

The mention of this possibility was delayed after the others, even after His saying: "Do you ask them for a reward," despite the apparent requirement that it should come before it, because it is the weakest and most remote of the possibilities.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَاصْبِرْ لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ وَلا تَكُنْ كَصاحِبِ الْحُوتِ إِذْ نادى وَهُوَ مَكْظُومٌ** — **So submit patiently to the judgment of your Lord, and do not be like the Man of the Fish when he called out as he was choking with grief.** (68:48). The "Man of the Fish" (صاحِبِ الْحُوتِ) is the Prophet Jonah (يونس), peace be upon him. The **مكظوم** (one choking with grief) is one who suppresses anger (كظم الغيظ) by swallowing it; hence it is interpreted as one stifled by sorrow when he finds no relief for his anger. The prohibition of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) from being like Jonah (عليه‌السلام) at the time of his calling out while full of sorrow is a prohibition against the cause that leads to a similar trial, which is distress of the heart and haste for the punishment to arrive.

The meaning is: "Be patient for the meeting with your Lord, that He may entice them gradually and grant them respite, and do not hasten the punishment for them due to their disbelief. Do not be like Jonah, becoming like him when he was filled with sorrow or anger, calling out with glorification and the admission of wrongdoing. That is, be patient and beware of being afflicted with something resembling his trial." His calling out was his saying in the belly of the fish: **لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنْتَ سُبْحانَكَ إِنِّي كُنْتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ** — **There is no god but You! Immaculate are You! I have indeed been among the wrongdoers!** (21:87), as in Surah al-Anbiya.

It has been said that the *lam* in "for the judgment of your Lord" (لِحُكْمِ رَبِّكَ) means "until," containing a threat to his people and a warning that Allah will soon judge between him and them. The previous view is more suited to the context of the preceding verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَوْ لا أَنْ تَدارَكَهُ نِعْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ لَنُبِذَ بِالْعَراءِ وَهُوَ مَذْمُومٌ** — **Had it not been for a blessing from his Lord that came to his rescue, he would surely have been cast on the bare shore, being blameworthy.** (68:49). This serves as the rationale for the preceding prohibition: "do not be like the Man of the Fish." **التدارك** (rescue/reaching) means catching up and joining. The "blessing" (النعمة) has been interpreted as the acceptance of repentance. **النبذ** (casting) is throwing. **العراء** (bare shore) is land not covered by a roof or vegetation. **الذم** (blame) is the opposite of praise.

The meaning is: "Had it not been that a blessing from his Lord reached him and caught up with him — which was that Allah accepted his repentance — he would have been thrown onto the bare land while being blamed for what he did."

It should not be said that this verse contradicts His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَوْ لا أَنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الْمُسَبِّحِينَ لَلَبِثَ فِي بَطْنِهِ إِلى يَوْمِ يُبْعَثُونَ** — **Had he not been one of those who glorify [Allah], he would surely have remained in its belly till the day they are resurrected.** (37:143-144). For the implication of that verse is that the requirement of his action was to remain in its belly until the Day of Resurrection, while the implication of this verse is that its requirement was to be cast on the bare shore while being blameworthy; these are two contradictory consequences that cannot coexist.

For it is said: The two verses speak of two different requirements, each having its own effect. The verse in *al-Saffat* mentions that he (peace be upon him) was constant in glorification (التسبيح), continuing in it throughout his life before his trial — which is His saying: "he was one of those who glorify" — and had it not been for that, he would have remained in its belly until the Day of Resurrection. The verse we are currently discussing indicates that the blessing — which is the acceptance of his repentance in the belly of the fish — encompassed him, so he was not cast on the bare shore while being blameworthy.

Thus, the combination of the two verses indicates that his departing in anger required that he remain in its belly until the Day of Resurrection, but his constant glorification before being swallowed and after it prevented that. It was then decreed that he be cast on the bare shore, and the requirement of his action was that he be cast while blameworthy, but the rescue of his Lord's blessing prevented that, so he was cast without being blameworthy. Rather, Allah chose him and made him among the righteous. Thus, there is no contradiction between the two verses.

It has been repeated in our previous discussions that the reality of "blessing" (النعمة) is *Wilayah* (الولاية), and based on that, another meaning is determined for His saying: "Had it not been for a blessing from his Lord that came to his rescue."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَاجْتَباهُ رَبُّهُ فَجَعَلَهُ مِنَ الصَّالِحِينَ** — **Then his Lord chose him and made him one of the righteous.** (68:50). The clarification of the meaning of "choosing" (الاجتباء) and "righteousness" (الصلاح) has preceded in our earlier discussions.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنْ يَكادُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَيُزْلِقُونَكَ بِأَبْصارِهِمْ لَمَّا سَمِعُوا الذِّكْرَ** — **Indeed the faithless almost strike you over with their eyes when they hear the Reminder.** (68:51). The word *in* (إِنْ) is the lightened form of the heavy *inna*. **الزلق** (slipping) is to stumble. **الإزلاق** (striking over) is to cause to stumble or to fell, which is a metonymy for killing and destroying.

The meaning is: Those who disbelieve almost felled you with their eyes when they heard the Reminder (الذكر).

What is meant by "striking him over with the eyes" and felling him with them — according to the majority of commentators — is the "evil eye" (الإصابة بالأعين). This is a type of psychological influence for which there is no rational proof to deny, and cases have been observed that are consistent with it. It has also been mentioned in narrations, so there is no reason to reject it.

It has also been said that the meaning is that they look at you, when they hear the Reminder — which is the Quran — from you, with a look full of enmity and hatred, almost killing you with the intensity of their gaze.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَيَقُولُونَ إِنَّهُ لَمَجْنُونٌ وَما هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعالَمِينَ** — **And they say, ‘He is indeed crazy.’ But it is nothing but a reminder for all the worlds.** (68:51-52). Their accusing him of madness when they heard the Reminder is proof that their intention was to label the Quran as being from the promptings of devils. Therefore, He refuted their statement by saying that the Quran is nothing but a reminder for all creation (العالمين).

Their statement "He is indeed crazy" was refuted at the beginning of the Surah by His saying: **ما أَنْتَ بِنِعْمَةِ رَبِّكَ بِمَجْنُونٍ** — **By the grace of your Lord, you are not crazy.** (68:2). Thus, the end of the Surah corresponds with its beginning.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain of narration from al-Husayn ibn Sa'id, from Abu al-Hasan (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَنْ ساقٍ وَيُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ** — "The day when the shank is bared, and they are summoned to prostrate." He said: **حجاب من نور يكشف فيقع المؤمنون سجدا ـ وتدمج أصلاب المنافقين فلا يستطيعون السجود** — "A veil of light is bared, and the believers fall down in prostration, while the backs of the hypocrites become rigid so they cannot prostrate."

In the same source, with its chain of narration from 'Ubayd ibn Zurarah, from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him). He said: I asked him about the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عن ساقٍ**. He said: **كشف إزاره عن ساقه فقال : سبحان ربي الأعلى** — "He pulled back his garment from his shank and said: 'Glory be to my Lord, the Most High.'"

I say: al-Saduq said after citing the hadith: "His saying: 'Glory be to my Lord, the Most High' is a declaration of Allah's transcendence (تنزيه) above having a shank." End quote. There is another narration with this meaning from al-Halabi from Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him).

In the same source, with its chain of narration from Mu'alla ibn Khunays. He said: I said to Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him): What is meant by His saying: **وَقَدْ كانُوا يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَى السُّجُودِ وَهُمْ سالِمُونَ**? He said: **وهم مستطيعون** — "While they were capable."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Bukhari, ibn al-Mundhir, and ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Sa'id: I heard the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying: **يكشف ربنا عن ساقه فيسجد له كل مؤمن ومؤمنة ، ويبقى من كان يسجد في الدنيا رياء وسمعة ـ فيذهب ليسجد فيعود ظهره طبقا واحدا** — "Our Lord will bare His shank, and every believing man and woman will prostrate to Him. There will remain those who used to prostrate in the world out of ostentation and seeking reputation; they will go to prostrate, but their backs will become a single rigid plate."

In the same source, ibn Mandah narrated in *al-Radd 'ala al-Jahmiyyah* from Abu Hurayrah. He said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said regarding **يَوْمَ يُكْشَفُ عَنْ ساقٍ**: **يكشف الله عن ساقه** — "Allah will bare His shank."

In the same source, Ishaq ibn Rahwayh narrated in his *Musnad*, as did 'Abd ibn Humayd, ibn Abi al-Dunya, al-Tabarani, al-Ajurri in *al-Shari'ah*, al-Daraqutni in *al-Ru'yah*, al-Hakim (who authenticated it), ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Ba'th*, from 'Abdullah ibn Mas'ud, from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). He said: Allah will gather the people on the Day of Resurrection, and Allah will descend in the shadows of the clouds. A caller will call out: "O people! Are you not satisfied with your Lord [Who] created you, fashioned you, and provided for you — that He should put every person among you in the charge of what he used to worship and follow in the world? Is that not justice from your Lord?" They will say: "Yes."

He said: Then every person among you will go to what he used to worship in the world, and what they used to worship in the world will be personified for them. For the one who used to worship Jesus, the devil of Jesus will be personified; for the one who used to worship Ezra, the devil of Ezra will be personified — until even the tree, the wood, and the stone are personified for them.

The people of Islam will remain kneeling. Then the Lord, the Mighty and Majestic, will manifest to them and say: "What is the matter with you that you do not go as the people have gone?" They will say: "We have a Lord Whom we have not yet seen." He will say: "By what will you recognize your Lord if you see Him?" They will say: "Between us and Him is a sign; if we see it, we will recognize Him." He will say: "What is it?" They will say: "He bares a shank."

At that, He will bare a shank, and everyone who used to prostrate willingly will fall down in prostration. There will remain a people whose backs are like the horns of cows; they will want to prostrate but will not be able to. (The hadith continues).

**I say:** These three narrations are based on anthropomorphism (التشبيه), which contradicts rational proofs and the explicit text of the Mighty Book; therefore, they are to be rejected or interpreted metaphorically.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Sufyan ibn al-Simt. He said: Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: **إن الله إذا أراد بعبد خيرا فأذنب ذنبا أتبعه بنقمة ـ وذكره الاستغفار ، فإذا أراد بعبد شرا فأذنب ذنبا أتبعه بنعمة ـ لينسيه الاستغفار ويتمادى بها ، وهو قول الله عز وجل : «سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ» بالنعم والمعاصي** — "When Allah intends good for a servant and he commits a sin, He follows it with an affliction and reminds him to seek forgiveness. But when He intends evil for a servant and he commits a sin, He follows it with a blessing to make him forget seeking forgiveness so that he may persist in it. This is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: 'We will draw them on gradually from whence they do not know' — through blessings and acts of disobedience."

**I say:** Some narrations regarding gradual enticement (الاستدراج) have already preceded under the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: **سَنَسْتَدْرِجُهُمْ مِنْ حَيْثُ لا يَعْلَمُونَ**, Ayah 182 of Surah al-A'raf.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِذْ نادى وَهُوَ مَكْظُومٌ**, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): He says: **مغموم** — "Grieved."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لَوْ لا أَنْ تَدارَكَهُ نِعْمَةٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ**. He said: **النعمة الرحمة** — "The blessing is mercy."

In the same source: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **لَنُبِذَ بِالْعَراءِ**. He said: **الموضع الذي لا سقف له** — "The place that has no roof."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنْ يَكادُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا**, al-Bukhari narrated from ibn 'Abbas that the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: **العين حق** — "The [evil] eye is a reality."

In the same source, Abu Nu'aym narrated in *al-Hilyah* from Jabir that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: **العين تدخل الرجل القبر والجمل القدر** — "The [evil] eye enters the man into the grave and the camel into the cooking pot."

**I say:** There are narrations that apply the preceding verses to the *Wilayah*; these are of the nature of "conceptual application" (الجري) rather than "exegesis" (التفسير), which is why we have not included them.
